sources.notes: Read the statement and constraints: c is a character-modified interleaving of a and b, with |c|=|a|+|b| and aggregate source lengths at most 2000 each.
The official English editorial revision and reference implementation confirm the two-prefix DP and mismatch-cost transitions; the prose widget itself was dynamically unavailable.
The Luogu solution independently formulates the equivalent maximum-match DP over used prefixes of a and b.
Re-derived the minimum-mismatch recurrence and exhaustively compared it with every interleaving for all binary a,b of lengths up to 3 and every target c; all cases matched.

annotation.summary: Find the minimum number of positions to change in c so that it becomes an interleaving of a and b while preserving the internal order of both source strings.
annotation.constraints: 1 <= |a|,|b| <= 1000, |c|=|a|+|b|, and the sums of |a| and |b| over test cases are each at most 2000; an O(|a||b|) DP is intended.
annotation.core_idea: Let dp[i][j] be the minimum mismatches after taking the first i characters of a and first j characters of b to form c[0..i+j). The next output position is fixed as i+j, so transition from dp[i-1][j] by taking a[i-1], or from dp[i][j-1] by taking b[j-1], adding one exactly when that character differs from c[i+j-1]. The answer is dp[|a|][|b|].
annotation.complexity: O(|a||b|) time and O(|a||b|) memory; rolling rows reduce memory to O(min(|a|,|b|)).
tricks: Use i+j to identify the forced position in c.
Replace exponential enumeration of interleavings by a lattice of prefix pairs.
Treat a replacement as a unit mismatch cost on each transition.

solution_variants.name: min-cost-interleaving-grid-dp
solution_variants.summary: Run shortest-path-style DP on the (i,j) prefix grid, where each right/down step chooses the next character from one source and pays its mismatch with c[i+j].
solution_variants.complexity: O(|a||b|) time and O(|a||b|) memory, or O(min(|a|,|b|)) memory with rolling rows

tags.evidence: The complete solution is a two-dimensional DP over the prefix lattice (i,j), with transitions from adjacent lattice cells.
The central object is a merge of two strings that preserves the internal order of each source, and the DP chooses which source supplies every next position.
tags.description: Problems where a target sequence is formed by interleaving two or more source sequences while preserving each source's internal order, typically solved by prefix-state dynamic programming.
tags.created_reason: The existing string/subsequence tag is broader and does not let users retrieve the classic order-preserving interleaving or shuffle DP pattern directly.

tags.evidence: In every feasible unmodified merge, a and b both appear as order-preserving subsequences of the resulting string.
